title: Film portfolio
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to the IB Film SL Film Portfolio, a compulsory internal assessment component. It deconstructs the assessment criteria, explores the five production roles, and offers strategies for creating high-scoring documentation that demonstrates inquiry, process, and critical reflection.
learningObjectives: Understand the requirements and assessment criteria for the Film Portfolio.
Identify the specific responsibilities and documentation needs for each of the five production roles.
Develop skills in critical reflection, moving beyond description to analytical evaluation of the filmmaking process.
Learn how to structure and present the 9-page portfolio document effectively to meet top-band criteria.
Analyse how to demonstrate meaningful connections between personal work and wider film theory, history, and filmmakers.

simpleExplanation.title: Your Filmmaker's Diary
simpleExplanation.summary: The Film Portfolio is not just about the final film you create; it's a detailed, reflective journal of your entire filmmaking journey. Examiners want to see your thought process, how you solved problems, and what you learned by taking on a specific production role.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your portfolio as a professional chef's recipe development notebook. It doesn't just contain the final, perfect recipe. It shows the initial idea, the different ingredients they tried, the cooking methods that failed, the breakthrough that made the dish work, and notes on why certain flavours and textures combine effectively. The process and the learning are as important as the final dish served.
steps: Select ONE specialist production role (e.g., Director, Editor) and clearly define your creative intentions.
Engage in detailed pre-production, documenting your research, planning, and how your ideas connect to established filmmakers or film movements.
Execute your role during production and post-production, systematically documenting challenges and creative decisions as they happen.
Compile your 9-page portfolio by curating your evidence and writing analytical reflections that justify your choices and evaluate your learning.

flashcards.back: An internal assessment component requiring students to perform one of five production roles in a 3-5 minute film and submit a 9-page portfolio documenting the process.
The single, specialist creative role a student must undertake. The five options are: Director, Cinematographer, Editor, Sound Designer, and Writer.
Assesses the completed 3-5 minute film itself, focusing on technical competence and the creative realisation of intentions. (Max 10 marks)
Assesses the 9-page documentation of the filmmaking process, evaluating planning, problem-solving, and reflection. (Max 15 marks)
Assesses the student's ability to connect their own work to wider film culture, including specific films, filmmakers, and theoretical concepts. (Max 5 marks)
Assesses the overall coherence, clarity, and organisation of the portfolio, ensuring it presents a clear narrative of the student's learning journey. (Max 5 marks)
A concise statement within the portfolio explaining the aims, intentions, and target audience for the film project.

Descriptive: 'We used a low-angle shot.' Analytical: 'To convey the character's dominance, we chose a low-angle shot, a technique inspired by Welles in Citizen Kane, which foreshortens the figure and positions the viewer in a subordinate role.'
Focuses on vision, interpretation of the script, guiding actors' performances, and ensuring a cohesive final film. Documentation includes annotated scripts, storyboards, and rehearsal notes.
Focuses on assembling footage, shaping rhythm and pace, and constructing meaning through juxtaposition. Documentation includes editing timelines, notes on continuity, and analysis of pacing choices.
Focuses on the visual realisation of the film, including lighting, framing, composition, and camera movement. Documentation includes lighting diagrams, shot lists, and lens choice justifications.
Focuses on the entire soundscape, including dialogue, foley, sound effects, and music. Documentation includes sound maps, cue sheets, and analysis of how sound creates mood.
Focuses on developing the screenplay. This role is completed *before* production begins. Documentation includes multiple script drafts, character biographies, and research into narrative structure.

faq.q: Can I choose more than one production role for my portfolio?
faq.a: No. The IB Film guide is explicit that you must choose and be assessed on only one specialist role. Your portfolio must be written entirely from the perspective of that single role, focusing on your specific contributions.

faq.q: What if my final film isn't very good? Will I fail?
faq.a: Not at all. The assessment is heavily weighted towards the portfolio pages (Criterion B, C, D), which document your process and learning. A 'perfect' film with a weak, descriptive portfolio will score much lower than a flawed film that is accompanied by a deeply analytical and reflective portfolio that explains the challenges and what was learned from them.

faq.q: How much text should I have versus images in the 9 pages?
faq.a: There is no magic ratio. The key is balance and purpose. Use images (storyboards, diagrams, screenshots) as evidence, but they are meaningless without concise, analytical text that explains their significance. Annotate your images directly. Avoid long, unbroken paragraphs of text and avoid pages that are just a collage of un-annotated pictures. Every element must serve the narrative of your learning.

faq.q: Do I need to work in a group?
faq.a: Yes, filmmaking is a collaborative process. You will typically create the film as part of a small group where each member takes on a different specialist role. Your portfolio, however, is an individual submission about your own work within that group.

sections.content: The Film Portfolio is the most hands-on component of the IB Film course, accounting for 35% of your SL grade. It is your opportunity to move from film analyst to filmmaker. Success in this task is not measured by producing a Hollywood-level blockbuster, but by demonstrating a deep, reflective, and academically rigorous engagement with one specific filmmaking role. This lesson will equip you with the tools to document your process in a way that satisfies the highest bands of the IB assessment criteria.
Understanding the four criteria is the first step to success. Your final mark is a composite of your practical work and your written documentation.

items: **Criterion A: Film Reel (10 marks)**: Assesses the technical proficiency and creative effectiveness of your 3-5 minute film. Examiners look for competence in areas like cinematography, sound, editing, and how well the final product realises the stated intentions.

**Criterion B: Portfolio Pages (15 marks)**: This is the core of the assessment. It evaluates your 9 pages of documentation. High marks are awarded for clear evidence of planning, thoughtful problem-solving, and insightful reflection on your specific production role.

**Criterion C: Connections to Film and Filmmakers (5 marks)**: This criterion assesses your ability to situate your work within a broader cinematic context. You must demonstrate how your choices were influenced by specific films, filmmakers, movements, or theoretical concepts you have studied.

**Criterion D: Holistic Presentation (5 marks)**: This evaluates the overall quality of your submission. Is it well-organised? Is the communication clear and concise? Does the portfolio present a coherent and compelling narrative of your creative journey?

sections.content: You must select and stick to ONE of the five production roles. Your portfolio must be written from the perspective of this role, focusing only on your specific contributions and challenges. Trying to document everything will dilute your focus and lower your mark in Criterion B.

items: **Director:** Your focus is on interpreting the script, guiding performance, and maintaining a cohesive artistic vision. Your portfolio will feature annotated scripts, storyboards, rehearsal notes, and reflections on managing the creative team.

**Cinematographer:** Your focus is on the visual language of the film. Your portfolio will be rich with visual evidence: lighting diagrams, floor plans, annotated frame grabs, and justifications for camera placement, movement, and lens choices.

**Editor:** Your focus is on rhythm, pace, and the construction of meaning in post-production. Your portfolio should include screenshots of your editing timeline, analysis of different cut options, and reflections on how your choices shaped the narrative and emotional impact.

**Sound Designer:** Your focus is on the entire aural world of the film. Your portfolio will contain sound maps, cue sheets, evidence of foley recording, and analysis of how diegetic and non-diegetic sound were used to create atmosphere and meaning.

**Writer:** Your focus is on the screenplay. This role is completed before filming. Your portfolio will show the evolution of the script through multiple drafts, character development notes, and research into genre conventions and narrative structures.

sections.question: Model Portfolio Entry: Cinematographer's Pre-Production Planning
sections.solution:
Below is an example of a high-level entry for a Cinematographer's portfolio, demonstrating planning and connection to film theory (Criterion B & C).

**Page 3: Lighting the Interrogation Scene**

**Intention:** To create a tense, psychologically oppressive atmosphere that visually isolates the protagonist, drawing inspiration from the chiaroscuro lighting of film noir, particularly the work of cinematographer John Alton in *The Big Combo* (1955).

**Plan & Justification:**
Unlike the high-key lighting used elsewhere in the film to denote normalcy, this scene will employ a low-key, single-source setup. A single hard light (a 650W fresnel) will be positioned high and to the side of the character (see Diagram A), creating deep shadows across their face. This 'hatchet' lighting will obscure one eye, symbolising their divided loyalties and moral ambiguity. The lack of fill light will produce a high-contrast image, plunging the background into darkness and trapping the character in a pool of light. This choice is a direct homage to Alton's philosophy that 'it's not what you light, it's what you don't light'. By denying the audience full visual information, we enhance the scene's psychological tension.

**(Image Below: A hand-drawn lighting diagram labelled 'Diagram A', showing the position of the camera, subject, and single key light).**

sections.content: The single biggest differentiator between a mediocre and an excellent portfolio is the quality of reflection. You must move beyond simply describing what you did and instead analyse *why* you did it, what challenges you faced, how you solved them, and what you learned. Use the 'What? So what? Now what?' framework.

items: **What?** (Description): 'During the shoot, we realised the dialogue was inaudible because of wind noise.'

**So what?** (Analysis): 'This rendered the scene unusable as the emotional core was lost. The initial plan to use only on-location sound proved naive for our equipment and environment. This forced me, as Sound Designer, to re-evaluate the scene's entire aural strategy.'

**Now what?** (Reflection & Learning): 'In post-production, I decided to embrace the challenge by creating a layered, subjective soundscape. I used ADR to re-record the dialogue cleanly, then mixed in a stylised, heightened wind effect to represent the character's internal turmoil, a technique inspired by Walter Murch's work in *The Conversation*. This problem led to a more powerful and thematically relevant sound design than originally planned.'

sections.content: Curate, don't just collect. The 9-page limit is strict. Do not simply dump all your notes into the document. Select the most compelling evidence of planning, problem-solving, and reflection. An annotated storyboard that shows a change made on set is far more valuable than three pages of unedited meeting minutes. Every image and sentence should have a purpose.

sections.question: Model Portfolio Entry: Editor's Post-Production Reflection
sections.solution:
This example demonstrates high-level reflection on a problem, linking it to editing principles and achieving top marks for Criterion B.

**Page 7: Solving the Pacing Problem in the Chase Sequence**

**Challenge:** The initial assembly of the chase sequence (01:45-02:10) felt sluggish and lacked tension. The shots, though visually interesting, did not build momentum. My initial edit, using shots of roughly equal length (approx. 3 seconds each), created a monotonous rhythm.

**(Image Below: A screenshot of the editing timeline, labelled 'Initial Assembly', showing evenly spaced clips).**

**Solution & Analysis:**
Reflecting on Eisenstein's theory of metric montage, I realised I needed to accelerate the cutting rhythm to heighten the audience's physiological response. I re-cut the sequence, starting with longer takes and progressively shortening the shot duration as the chase intensifies. The final ten seconds of the sequence are comprised of 15 separate shots, some lasting only 4-5 frames.

**(Image Below: A screenshot of the editing timeline, labelled 'Final Cut', showing clips becoming progressively shorter).**

**Learning:** This process was a practical lesson in the physical power of editing. It wasn't the content of the shots that was the problem, but their temporal relationship. By manipulating the pace of the cuts, I was able to generate suspense and excitement from the same set of footage. This confirmed my understanding that an editor's primary role is not merely to assemble, but to construct and manipulate time to create emotional impact.

sections.content: There is no single mandatory structure, but a logical flow is essential for Criterion D (Holistic Presentation). A clear narrative that follows the chronology of the filmmaking process is often most effective. Consider the following as a template:

items: **Page 1: Rationale & Vision.** Who are you in this project (your role)? What is the film about? What are your key artistic goals and influences (Criterion C)?

**Pages 2-4: Pre-production.** Evidence of your planning. For a Director, this is annotated scripts/storyboards. For a Cinematographer, lighting tests/diagrams. For an Editor, research into pacing in your chosen genre.

**Pages 5-6: Production & Problem-Solving.** Show, don't just tell. Use annotated on-set photos or frame grabs to illustrate a challenge you faced in your role and how you overcame it. This is a prime area for reflection.

**Pages 7-8: Post-production & Evaluation.** For Editors and Sound Designers, this is your main section. For others, it's about evaluating the final product against your initial intentions. What worked? What didn't? Why?

**Page 9: Final Reflection & Works Cited.** A summative reflection on your learning journey in this role. What are your key takeaways as a filmmaker? Also, include a properly formatted list of all films, books, and articles cited in your portfolio.
